title: Benchmarking in CV
concept: Benchmarking is the process of evaluating different models on a standardized dataset to compare their performance.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary metric used to evaluate detection models in PASCAL VOC?
exercises.explain: Mean Average Precision (mAP) is the standard metric for assessing object detection models across classes.

cards.title: The Leaderboard Race
cards.body: Every week, new computer vision models claim to be the 'state-of-the-art' (SOTA). How do we know if these claims are true? We run the models through standardized benchmark tests to verify their speed, accuracy, and efficiency.

cards.title: Benchmarking
cards.body: Benchmarking is the process of testing multiple models on a standardized, fixed dataset using identical evaluation metrics and hardware configurations to rank their performance.

cards.title: The Formula 1 Track
cards.body: To see which car is fastest, you don't race them on random streets. You put them on the same race track (benchmark dataset) with the same curves and measuring sensors, isolating the car design from external factors.

cards.title: Papers With Code
cards.body: Platforms like 'Papers With Code' host public leaderboards. For the COCO test set, models are ranked by their mAP. Researchers submit their model code and predictions to prove their model is the new SOTA.

cards.title: Test Set Leakage
cards.body: You might think benchmark scores are always honest. A major issue is data leakage: if test set images accidentally leak into the training batch, the model will get a fake, inflated high score.

cards.title: SOTA Tradeoffs
cards.body: Understanding how leaderboards can hide engineering realities:

left.text:
Highest accuracy mAP.
Often massive, slow ensemble.
Too expensive for real-world devices.

right.text:
Slightly lower accuracy.
Fast, lightweight, optimized.
Fits on mobile and edge cameras.

exercises.prompt: What does 'SOTA' stand for in machine learning research?
exercises.explain: SOTA stands for State-of-the-Art, representing the best-performing models on a benchmark at the current time.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Data leakage occurs when a model is evaluated on images it has already seen during its training phase.
exercises.explain: True. Data leakage occurs when validation or test set information leaks into the training pipeline, leading to over-optimistic evaluation scores.
SOTA models are often massive and have high inference latency, making them impractical for real-time edge use.
exercises.sentence: A model that scores the highest on a benchmark might be too slow for real-world production due to high ___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a risk when evaluating models solely on public benchmark datasets?
exercises.explain: Models can overfit to a benchmark's specific distribution (e.g., lighting, classes), failing on other real-world data.

options.text: Overfitting to the specific characteristics and biases of the benchmark dataset
Converting the model format to ONNX automatically
Reducing the number of training parameters in the neural network

exercises.prompt: You are hired to build a license plate reader for a toll booth. The current SOTA model on COCO has 99% accuracy but runs at 1 FPS. What should you do?

options.text: Prioritize a faster, lightweight detector (e.g., YOLO) that runs at 30 FPS, even if its COCO mAP is slightly lower than the slow SOTA model.
Deploy the 1 FPS model and ask drivers to pause at the toll booth for 5 seconds.
Train the 1 FPS model for more epochs to see if it speeds up.
Discard benchmarking entirely and write manual rules to detect characters.

exercises.explain: In production, throughput and latency are critical. A slightly less accurate model that runs in real-time is far more useful than a slow SOTA model. Next up: how we annotate datasets!
